coredata.dc:title: Red emission carbon dots for mitoxantrone detection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, Yujia
Chen, Anli
Yin, Xinghang
Li, Renjie
Deng, Qunfen
Yang, Rui
coredata.dc:description: Red-emitting carbon dots were designed for the first time as the fluorescent probe for the determination of mitoxantrone (MITX) anticancer drug in human serum based on the inner filter effect (IFE). With the increase of MITX concentration, the single emission fluorescence intensity of the red carbon dots (R-CDs) at 655 nm gradually decreased and the emission peak position red-shifted to 661 nm. The developed R-CDs detection probe was sensitive to MITX with a wide linear detection range of 0.096–30 µM and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.032 µM. Moreover, the proposed method had been successfully applied to the determination of MITX in human serum samples, showing exhibited good operability, low cost, and high accuracy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Red-emitting carbon dots
Mitoxantrone
Inner filter effect

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by the Program for Chongqing Natural Science Foundation of China ( cstc2019jcyj-msxmX0489 ). Special thanks to Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for help with the TEM analysis, and eceshi (www.eceshi.cn) for the XPS analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM of R-CDs, the inset is HRTEM (upper right) and histogram of particle size distribution (bottom right). (B) XRD. (C) XPS full scan spectrum. (D) C1s. (E) N1s. (F) O1s.
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs at excitation wavelengths from 490 to 580 nm. (B) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of R-CDs.
Influencing factors: (A) pH value (B) ionic strength (C) irradiation time (D) heating (E) cooling (F) heating-cooling cycles.

(A) Quenched fluorescence spectra of four anthracyclines on R-CDs. (B) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs after the addition of different concentrations of MITX (0–30 μM). (C) A linear relationship between the value of Ln(F 0/F 1) and the concentration of MITX.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of R-CDs and MITX and theoretical and experimental spectra of mixtures of R-CDs and MITX. (B) FL lifetime decay curves of R-CDs with and without MITX. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of MITX, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of R-CDs. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of R-CDs introduced into MITX, the concentrations of MITX were 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 μM. Inset: calibration curve of absorption values and concentration of MITX.
Effect of potential interferents on R-CDs and MITX. Concentrations of R-CDs, MITX and eighteen interferents were 0.125 mg·mL−1, 5 μM and 500 μM, respectively.
Synthesis of R-CDs and schematic diagram for determination of mitoxantrone.
Comparison of this work with other methods for MITX detection.
Determination of MITX in human serum.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ND: Not detected; RSD: Relative standard deviation

head.ce:title: Red emission carbon dots for mitoxantrone detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, No.2 Tiansheng Road, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, No.2 Tiansheng Road, Chongqing 400715, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red-emitting carbon dots were designed for the first time as the fluorescent probe for the determination of mitoxantrone (MITX) anticancer drug in human serum based on the inner filter effect (IFE). With the increase of MITX concentration, the single emission fluorescence intensity of the red carbon dots (R-CDs) at 655 nm gradually decreased and the emission peak position red-shifted to 661 nm. The developed R-CDs detection probe was sensitive to MITX with a wide linear detection range of 0.096–30 µM and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.032 µM. Moreover, the proposed method had been successfully applied to the determination of MITX in human serum samples, showing exhibited good operability, low cost, and high accuracy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Anthracyclines [1,2], including mitoxantrone, epirubicin, erythromycin, and emodin, are widely used in the treatment of cancer, with the advantages of the broad anti-tumor spectrum, strong anti-tumor effect, and precise efficacy. Among them, mitoxantrone (MITX) has high antitumor activity and less toxic side effects, showing high efficacy in the treatment of common malignant tumors such as malignant lymphoma, breast cancer, and acute leukemia [3–6]. When treated, MITX can widely distributed in the body by intravenous injection. However, long-term uncontrolled use of MITX will lead to its prolonged retention in the body, which can inhibit bone marrow due to slow excretion and cause serious side effects, such as leukopenia and thrombocytopenia [7]. Clinical studies have shown that when the concentration of MITX exceeds the upper limit of the allowable concentration level, MITX can lead to anemia, secondary myeloid leukemia, and heart disease [8]. Therefore, accurate quantitative and low-cost monitoring of the concentration of MITX in the blood has important meaning during treatment and toxicity control. Up to now, high-performance liquid chromatography [9,10], capillary zone electrophoresis [11,12], chemiluminescence [12,13], resonance Rayleigh scattering [14], electrochemistry [15–17] and fluorescence [4,18–20] have been successfully used for the detection of MITX. Among them, the fluorescence analysis method has high sensitivity and easy operation, which can meet the needs of highly demanding assays. Although fluorometric methods have been researched for the determination of MITX, the development of simple, sensitive, and accurate fluorescent probes for MITX remains a challenging task.

It is worth noting that, carbon dots (CDs), as a kind of fluorescent nanoprobe of great interest, has never been reported for the quantitative detection of MITX. CDs are a new type of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with spherical structures less than 10 nm in diameter. CDs are not only simple to prepare, easy to functionalization, and low cost, but also have outstanding advantages such as stable fluorescence properties, good biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity. Therefore, they are widely used in various fields, such as photocatalysis, sensing, analytical detection, cell imaging, and biomedicine [21–26]. Currently, the synthesized CDs are still dominated by ultraviolet excitation and emit blue-green wavelengths, but the background effects on biological samples limit their applications in bio-related fields. In contrast, red carbon points (R-CDs) with emission wavelengths greater than 600 nm can be excited by long wavelength light, have good tissue penetration, and consistently exhibit high photobleaching resistance in the red/near-infrared region, making them of high biomedical value. Thus, it is of great significance to develop simple and reliable R-CDs as fluorescent probes for the determination of MITX content in serum.

In this work, R-CDs are synthesized in N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) solvent using the solvothermal method with citric acid (CA) as the carbon source and formamide (FA) as the nitrogen source. The synthesized R-CDs are pollution-free, water-soluble, and well-dispersed. The experimental investigations reveal that the R-CDs have a wide temperature response range (Temperature10–60 ℃), while the detection of MITX is achieved based on the inner filter effect (IFE) ( Scheme 1). This investigation method has the advantages of fast response, high sensitivity, and wide linear range compared with other methods for MITX detection, and has broad application prospects in clinical diagnosis, biotechnology, and drug analysis. The red emission characteristics of R-CDs also provide the possibility for its application in fluorescence imaging.

Citric Acid and formamide were bought from Macklin. N, N-dimethylformamide was purchased from Aladdin. Mitoxantrone was obtained from Shanghai Dipper. Acetone was supplied by Chongqing Chuandong Chemical. All chemicals used in the experiments were of analytical purity specifications. All reagents in the experiment were dissolved in ultrapure water.

Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded by UV-2007 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were measured using FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded on TENSOR 27010409 spectrophotometer (Brucker, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained from Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were determined by K-Alpha Spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). The crystal structures of R-CDs were identified by SHIMADZU 7000 X-Ray Diffractometer (XRD) (Spectrum Analysis General, Beijing, China). Lifetime was obtained by Fluorolog-3 steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba, America).

R-CDs were synthesized according to the method reported by our group [27]. 1 g CA, 1.4 mL FA and 10 mL DMF were stirred thoroughly in a beaker. Then, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon lined reaction vessel and placed into a constant temperature drying oven at 180 °C and held for 4 h. After that, the reacted solution was centrifuged at 8000 r·min−1 for 10 min to remove insoluble particles. It was collected by adding a large amount of acetone as eluent until there was no fluorescence in the supernatant. Finally, the product was dissolved in water, and R-CDs were collected by vacuum drying at 60 ℃.

500 μL R-CDs (500 μg/mL) and 500 μL Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution [20] and different volumes of MITX (0.2 mM) work solutions or sample solutions were mixed evenly. Then, the final volume of the mixture was adjusted to 2 mL with distilled water to ensure that the final concentration of MITX ranged from 0 to 30 µM. The whole experiment was carried out at room temperature. Keeping the excitation and emission slit width all at 2 nm, the fluorescence intensity was recorded in the emission wavelength range from 565 to 800 nm at the optimal excitation wavelength of 560 nm. All fluorescence intensities were detected under the same conditions to ensure the accuracy of the experiments. The whole procedure was duplicated 5 times.

Human blood samples were provided by healthy volunteers from the Southwest University Hospital and the blood samples were centrifuged at 8000 r·min−1 for 10 min to separate serum [28,29]. In order to reduce matrix effects, the serum samples were diluted 100 times without pretreatment.

The morphology and particle size distribution of R-CDs were observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1A, R-CDs are dispersed spherical particles with good dispersion and small particle sizes. The interplanar spacing of 0.22 nm was clearly visible in HRTEM, corresponding to the lattice plane of graphene (100) [30,31]. One hundred nanoparticles were selected to measure their particle size. The particle size distribution of R-CDs ranged from 1.8 to 3.4 nm, with an average particle size of 2.6 nm. XRD pattern of R-CDs showed the appearance of a distinct sharp characteristic peak at 26.5° (Fig. 1B), indicating that R-CDs had a high degree of crystallinity. The surface functional groups and chemical composition of R-CDs were analyzed by FT-IR and XPS. As presented in Fig. S1, the absorption peaks at 3424 and 3190 cm−1 were contributed to O-H and N-H stretching vibrations, respectively [32,33]. 1710, 1632 and 1587 cm−1 were related to the stretching vibrations of CO, CN and CC [34]. the peaks located at 1355 and 1190 cm−1 were attributed to C-N and C-O stretching vibrations [35]. There were a large number of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of R-CDs, which enhanced their hydrophilicity. From the full spectrum of XPS, it can be seen that 284.17 eV, 399.21 eV, and 530.92 eV correspond to the three typical peaks of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. It was proved that R-CDs were composed of C, N, and O elements [36], with contents of 63.42%, 13.74%, and 22.84%, respectively (Fig. 1C). When analyzed by high-resolution XPS analysis, C1s can be divided into five peaks. The binding energies at 284.2, 285.2, 286.2, 287.2 and 288.5 eV corresponded to C-C/CC, C-N, C-O, CO/CN, -COOH bonds (Fig. 1D). N1s contained two peaks at 398.9 and 400.9 eV binding energies assigned to C-N and N-H bonds (Fig. 1E). Two peaks at 531.2 and 532.8 eV binding energies were found in O1s, which were ascribed to CO and C-O bonds (Fig. 1F). The data of FTIR and XPS were corroborated with each other to make sure the correct analysis results.

Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were measured to study the optical properties of R-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2A, the emission wavelength of R-CDs showed a typical excitation-dependent behavior. During the excitation wavelength varied from 490 to 580 nm, the maximum emission wavelength increased from 590 nm to 655 nm, with a Stokes shift of 65 nm. Fig. 2B showed the UV–vis absorption spectra, excitation spectra, and emission spectra of R-CDs. Four absorption peaks of R-CDs were present at 240, 340, 465, and 550 nm. Among them, the absorption peaks at 300–400 nm were attributed to π-π * transitions of aromatic conjugation systems (sp2 domains). 400–600 nm absorption peaks were attributed to n-π * transitions of aromatic sp2 bonds [32,36]. The maximum fluorescence was obtained at 560 nm excitation, so 560 nm and 655 nm were chosen as the best excitation and emission wavelengths for the experiment, respectively. Using Rhodamine 6 G as a reference, the measured relative fluorescence quantum yield of R-CDs was 26.8% (Fig. S2).

The effects of different pH values, ion concentration, ultraviolet lamp irradiation time at room temperature conditions, and different temperature conditions on the stability of R-CDs were studied. First of all, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs increased gradually from pH 2.0–6.0, slightly decreased from pH 6.0–8.0, and significantly increased in the range of pH 8.0–12.0, as shown in Fig. 3A, and the color of R-CDs did not change with the pH (Fig. S3). It was speculated that under acidic conditions, the protonation of carboxyl groups on the surface of R-CDs leads to a decrease in electrostatic repulsion, also brings about R-CDs aggregation and precipitation making the fluorescence intensity decrease; under alkaline conditions, the deprotonation of carboxyl groups on the surface of R-CDs increases the electrostatic repulsion, stabilizing the R-CDs solution and increasing the fluorescence intensity [37]. And then, as presented in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs decreased slightly with increasing NaCl concentration value, but the overall fluorescence intensity did not change significantly, indicating good salt tolerance. What’s more, after continuously irradiated R-CDs for one hour, the fluorescence intensity at 655 nm remained almost the same, which demonstrated that R-CD have good photostability (Fig. 3C). In addition, the fluorescence emission intensities were measured at different temperatures to further investigate the thermal stability of R-CDs. The results were shown in Figs. 3D and 3E. It was able to observe that the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs increased with the rise of temperature and decreased with the fall of temperature, showing a good linear relationship, so it showed the potential for temperature sensing. Meanwhile, the change of fluorescence spectra of R-CDs heated and cooled between 10 and 60 °C were shown in Fig. S4, and the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs maintained steady after five consecutive repeated cycles (Fig. 3F), indicating that the temperature bidirectional conversion process of R-CDs was well reversible.

The fluorescence spectra of probe R-CDs in the presence of anthracyclines were shown in Fig. 4 A, respectively. The addition of other anthracyclines such as epirubicin, erythromycin, and emodin induced much less change in the fluorescence spectra, while mitoxantrone showed the most obvious change. Specific spectral data could be found in Fig. S5. Therefore, hiring mitoxantrone as a representative, the performance of the R-CDs fluorescent probe was investigated.

The pH value, reaction temperature, and reaction time of the reaction system were optimized, where, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs before and after the addition of MITX was F 0 and F 1, respectively, and ΔF= F 0 - F 1. As shown in Fig. S6A, under alkaline conditions, the addition of MITX caused a large change in fluorescence intensity but poor stability, meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of the probe itself was unstable too, so the alkaline condition was not suitable for measurement. When pH was 6.0, the change in fluorescence intensity of the reaction system was the most obvious and very stable. Therefore, the optimal pH of the reaction system was 6.0. It could be seen in Fig. S6B, ΔF increased slightly with increasing temperature, but the overall changed very little. Further studies showed that the linear equations obtained at 25 ℃ and 50 ℃ with R-CDs were in good agreement (Fig. S6D), while ΔF caused by MITX was basically the same under different temperatures (Fig. S6E), indicating that temperature had little effect on the determination of MITX. Therefore, the room temperature was chosen for measurement. In addition, this reaction was very rapid, which can be completed within 1 min, and the reaction time did not affect the detection of MITX by the R-CDs probe (Fig. S6C).

Under the optimal experimental conditions, the R-CDs were mixed with different concentrations of MITX working solution and the fluorescence intensity was recorded at 655 nm. As the MITX concentration increased from 0 to 30 μM, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs was gradually quenched (Fig. 4B). Moreover, there was a good linear relationship between Ln(F 0/F 1) value and concentration of MITX within the range of 0.096–30 μmol·L−1. The linear fitting equation was: Ln(F 0/F 1) = 0.1164c-0.001210. The correlation coefficient (R2) was 0.9981 (Fig. 4 C). The calculated limit of detection (LOD) was 0.032 μmol·L−1. The designed fluorescent probe was green, inexpensive, easy to prepare, and can detect MITX rapidly and sensitively without any modification. This method had a wider linear range compared to previously reported literature for the detection of MITX ( Table 1).

A series of experiments were performed to understand the quenching mechanism of R-CDs fluorescence produced by MITX. Fig. 5A displayed that the original UV absorption peaks of R-CDs were not shifted and no new peaks appeared when MITX was added to the R-CDs, suggesting static quenching had not occurred. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs was 7.35 ns and 7.57 ns, in the presence and absence of MITX, respectively, indicating that dynamic quenching did not occur too [38,39] (Fig. 5B). Noted that the UV absorption spectrogram of MITX showed characteristic absorption peaks at 608 and 660 nm, which overlapped well with the excitation and emission peaks of R-CDs, and it was speculated that the fluorescence quenching mechanism might be related to IFE [40–42] (Fig. 5C). In the end, we found that UV–vis absorbance value of MITX at 565 nm increased with its concentration, with a good linear relationship (Fig. 5D). This relationship revealed that the enhanced competitive absorption of excitation light source by MITX resulted in a decrease in the actual energy acting on R-CDs, so the fluorescence emission intensity of R-CDs was reduced, further demonstrating that IFE caused the fluorescence quenching of R-CDs.

The fluorescence intensity of R-CDs was determined by replacing MITX or co-existing with MITX with different types of possible interfering substances, including mental ions (Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ag+, Ba2+), amino acid (histidine, lysine, threonine, glutamate) and other co-existing substances (creatinine, uracil, cytosine, adenine, dopamine, ascorbic acid, and hemoglobin). In the presence of interfering substances, as shown in Fig. 6, the yellow and the green histograms respectively indicated the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs in the absence and presence of MITX. It could be seen that the interference substances had little effect on the fluorescence of R-CDs and the R-CDs-MITX system, indicating that R-CDs had high selectivity and anti-interference ability when used as a fluorescent probe to determine MITX.

Human serum samples obtained from healthy volunteers were diluted 100 times as the actual samples. The accuracy of the fluorescent probe was tested by the standard addition method. The analytical data were listed in Table 2. The values of recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) confirm the accuracy and feasibility of R-CDs, which ranged from 94.5% to 106.5% and 0.7–1.8%. Thus, the R-CDs fluorescence probe can be used as a novel method to monitor MITX in clinical studies.

In summary, R-CDs were synthesized by a simple and eco-friendly solvothermal method. Based on the mechanism of IFE between R-CDs and MITX, the novel label-free fluorescent nanoprobe for the rapid and accurate detection of MITX was designed and was successfully used to detect MITX in human serum samples in vitro for the first time. In fact, this method had a wide detection range and strong anti-interference ability, and could detect MITX quickly and sensitively, showing great potential in clinical diagnosis and drug analysis.

Yu-Jia Zhong: Conceptualization; Data curation; Formal analysis; Investigation; Writing – original draft; Writing - review & editing. An-Li Chen: Data curation; Formal analysis; Investigation. Xing-Hang Yin: Conceptualization; Investigation. Ren-Jie Li: Data curation; Investigation. Qun-Fen Deng: Data curation; Investigation. Rui Yang: Funding acquisition; Resources; Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the Program for Chongqing Natural Science Foundation of China (cstc2019jcyj-msxmX0489). Special thanks to Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for help with the TEM analysis, and eceshi (www.eceshi.cn) for the XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.133535.

ce:section.ce:para:

.
